description: The real thing. Smoky, deeply seasoned, party-style jollof rice cooked the traditional way — long-fried tomato base, layered spices, and that iconic bottom-pot char.

ingredients.name: parboiled long grain rice
ingredients.note: Parboiled only. Regular rice will go mushy. Wash until water runs clear.

ingredients.name: plum tomatoes
ingredients.note: Fresh is better for party jollof. Canned works but fry longer.

ingredients.name: red bell pepper (tatashe)
ingredients.note: This gives the deep red colour and mild sweetness. Don't skip.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Adjust to your heat tolerance. 1 for mild, 3 for real party heat.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 2 go into the blended base, 1 sliced for frying the base.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: Or any neutral oil. Don't reduce this — the oil carries the flavour.

ingredients.name: chicken stock
ingredients.note: Homemade is best. Cube dissolved in water is acceptable. Beef stock also works.

ingredients.name: tomato paste
ingredients.note: Deepens the colour and intensifies tomato flavour. Don't skip.

ingredients.name: bay leaves
ingredients.note: Adds background depth. Remove before serving.

ingredients.name: curry powder
ingredients.note: Nigerian curry powder (yellow tin). This is not optional for party jollof flavour.

ingredients.name: thyme
ingredients.note: Dried is fine. Fresh thyme (2 sprigs) also works.

ingredients.name: white pepper
ingredients.note: Adds warmth without visible specks. Black pepper is a fine substitute.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: Gives that authentic umami depth. Skip if unavailable — dish still works.

ingredients.name: seasoning cubes
ingredients.note: Maggi or Knorr chicken cubes. Crush before adding.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season in layers — once at the base, once before adding rice.

ingredients.name: chicken pieces
ingredients.note: For proper party jollof. Parboil or grill the chicken and use the stock. Skip for vegetarian.

ingredients.name: foil / heavy lid
ingredients.note: Critical. You need a tight seal to trap steam. Foil under the lid is the secret to smoky party jollof.

steps.title: Prepare the Tomato Base
steps.instruction: Blend plum tomatoes, red bell peppers, scotch bonnet, and 2 of the onions together until very smooth. You should have a thick, bright red liquid. Do not add water — the vegetables have enough moisture. Slice the remaining onion into rings and set aside.
steps.sensory_cue: Deep red, smooth, thick liquid. Should smell fresh and sharp. No chunks.
steps.failure_risk: If the blend is watery, strain it briefly or you will spend much longer frying it down. Too much water = weak base.

steps.title: Fry the Sliced Onions
steps.instruction: Heat the oil in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium-high heat. Add the sliced onion rings. Fry, stirring occasionally, until they are soft, translucent, and beginning to turn golden at the edges. This builds the first flavour layer.
steps.sensory_cue: Onions should look soft, slightly golden, and smell sweet — not raw and sharp. The oil should shimmer around them.
steps.failure_risk: Don't burn the onions. Burnt onion at this stage ruins the entire base — it will taste bitter throughout.

steps.title: Add Tomato Paste and Fry
steps.instruction: Add the tomato paste to the fried onions. Stir it in and fry for 3–4 minutes, stirring constantly, until it darkens slightly and smells cooked — not raw. This step removes the tinny taste from the paste.
steps.sensory_cue: Paste should darken from bright red to a deeper brick-red. Smell shifts from sharp/tinny to rich and caramelised.
steps.failure_risk: Under-frying the paste leaves a tinny, raw tomato flavour in the finished dish. You must cook it out.

steps.title: Pour In Blended Mix and Fry the Base
steps.instruction: Pour the blended tomato and pepper mix into the pot. Stir to combine with the paste. Increase heat to high. Fry this mix, stirring every 90 seconds, until the liquid is significantly reduced, the colour has deepened to a darker red-orange, and the oil has fully risen to the surface. This step is the heart of party jollof — do not rush it.
steps.sensory_cue: The base should look thick and paste-like — not liquid. Oil should be visibly floating on top. Colour: deep brick-red to dark orange. The raw tomato smell should be completely gone, replaced by a rich, fried aroma.
steps.failure_risk: This is the most common failure point. If you don't fry long enough, the base will be watery, the rice will cook unevenly, and the final dish will taste raw and sour. The oil rising is your confirmation signal.

steps.title: Season the Base
steps.instruction: Reduce heat to medium. Add curry powder, thyme, white pepper, ground crayfish, crushed seasoning cubes, and salt. Stir everything in thoroughly. Taste the base — it should taste well-seasoned, almost slightly over-salted (the rice will absorb salt). Add bay leaves and stir in.
steps.sensory_cue: Base should smell complex, layered — you should clearly detect the curry and thyme. Colour shifts to a warm, deep orange-red. Taste it — bold and full is correct.
steps.failure_risk: Under-seasoning at this stage cannot be fixed later once the rice goes in. Be bold here.

steps.title: Add Chicken Stock
steps.instruction: Pour in the chicken stock. Stir to combine with the seasoned base. Bring everything to a rolling boil over medium-high heat. Taste again and adjust salt if needed. The liquid should be deeply flavoured.
steps.sensory_cue: The pot should be bubbling actively. Colour: rich orange-red. Aroma: full, savoury, complex — like the dish is already done.
steps.failure_risk: Cold or weak stock dilutes all the work you did on the base. Use hot stock if possible. If the stock seems bland, add another seasoning cube now.

steps.title: Add the Rice
steps.instruction: Add the washed parboiled rice to the boiling base. Stir well so every grain is coated in the sauce. The liquid should just about cover the rice — if it doesn't quite reach, add a small splash of stock or hot water. Do not add too much liquid. Stir once more, then reduce heat to low.
steps.sensory_cue: Rice should look evenly coated — every grain stained red-orange. Liquid should be at or just below the surface of the rice, not drowning it.
steps.failure_risk: Too much liquid = soggy, wet jollof. Too little = rice won't cook through before the liquid dries. The correct level is rice just at the surface of the liquid.

steps.title: Seal and Steam — First Cook
steps.instruction: Cover the pot tightly. For party jollof, place foil directly on the surface of the rice before putting the lid on — this traps steam inside and creates the smoky effect. Cook on low heat. Do NOT open the lid for the first 20 minutes. Let the steam do the work.
steps.sensory_cue: You should see steam escaping from the edges of the lid. The base of the pot will begin to smell slightly smoky after 15 minutes — this is intentional. That's the party jollof bottom forming.
steps.failure_risk: Lifting the lid releases steam and causes uneven cooking. Trust the process. The only exception: if you smell burning (not smoky — burning), lower the heat immediately.

steps.title: Check, Fold and Finish
steps.instruction: After 20 minutes, open the lid carefully (steam is hot). Check the rice — it should be almost done, just slightly firm in the centre. Gently fold the rice from the bottom up using a wooden spoon, bringing the bottom layer to the top. Re-seal with foil and lid. Cook for a final 10–15 minutes on the lowest heat setting.
steps.sensory_cue: The bottom layer should have a thin, slightly darkened crust — this is the smoky party bottom. It should smell deeply savoury and slightly charred. Folding distributes this flavour through the whole pot. Final rice should be fully cooked, fluffy, not clumping.
steps.failure_risk: Don't over-fold — you'll break the rice grains and create a mushy texture. One gentle fold from bottom to top is all you need.

steps.title: Rest and Serve
steps.instruction: Turn off the heat. Keep the pot sealed and let the jollof rest for 5–10 minutes before serving. This allows the steam to redistribute and the rice to settle. Remove the bay leaves. Fluff gently with a fork before plating.
steps.sensory_cue: Rice should look fluffy, each grain separate and stained a rich orange-red. The aroma rising from the pot should be smoky, deeply savoury, and complex. This is the moment.
steps.failure_risk: Serving immediately without resting causes the rice to stick together and steam escapes unevenly. Five minutes of rest makes a real difference.
